title: Stability across folds; importance ≠ causality
scope: Two critical caveats when interpreting feature evaluation metrics. High importance doesn't guarantee the feature is consistently useful, nor does it imply a causal mechanism.
key_concepts: Evaluating feature importance on a single train-test split is vulnerable to variance.
A reliable feature maintains consistent importance rankings across all cross-validation folds.
A feature with high predictive utility merely has a strong mathematical correlation with the target; it does not necessarily cause the target outcome.
definitions: Cross-validation stability: The degree to which a feature's calculated importance remains constant across different partitions of the training data.
examples: Umbrella sales are highly predictive of traffic accidents, but banning umbrellas won't stop accidents. The model exploits the correlation, but there is no causality.
relations_to_other_chapters: Points forward to Ch 17 regarding making actionable decisions from ML models.
what_to_exclude: Causal inference frameworks like do-calculus or instrumental variables.
